infobox.value: (**The Academy**) is one of the oldest [learned societies](/wiki/Learned_societies "Learned societies") in the [United States](/wiki/United_States_of_America "United States of America"). It was founded in 1780 during the [American Revolution](/wiki/American_Revolution "American Revolution") by [John Adams](/wiki/John_Adams "John Adams"), [John Hancock](/wiki/John_Hancock "John Hancock"), [James Bowdoin](/wiki/James_Bowdoin "James Bowdoin"),[[2]](#cite_note-2) [Andrew Oliver](/wiki/Andrew_Oliver_(1731%E2%80%931799) "Andrew Oliver (1731–1799)"), and other [Founding Fathers of the United States](/wiki/Founding_Fathers_of_the_United_States "Founding Fathers of the United States").[[3]](#cite_note-3) It is headquartered in [Cambridge, Massachusetts](/wiki/Cambridge,_Massachusetts "Cambridge, Massachusetts").

The **American Academy of Arts and Sciences** (**The Academy**) is one of the oldest [learned societies](/wiki/Learned_societies "Learned societies") in the [United States](/wiki/United_States_of_America "United States of America"). It was founded in 1780 during the [American Revolution](/wiki/American_Revolution "American Revolution") by [John Adams](/wiki/John_Adams "John Adams"), [John Hancock](/wiki/John_Hancock "John Hancock"), [James Bowdoin](/wiki/James_Bowdoin "James Bowdoin"),[[2]](#cite_note-2) [Andrew Oliver](/wiki/Andrew_Oliver_(1731%E2%80%931799) "Andrew Oliver (1731–1799)"), and other [Founding Fathers of the United States](/wiki/Founding_Fathers_of_the_United_States "Founding Fathers of the United States").[[3]](#cite_note-3) It is headquartered in [Cambridge, Massachusetts](/wiki/Cambridge,_Massachusetts "Cambridge, Massachusetts").

Membership in the academy is achieved through a nominating petition, review, and election process.[[4]](#cite_note-4) The academy's quarterly journal, *[Dædalus](/wiki/Daedalus_(journal) "Daedalus (journal)")*, is published by the [MIT Press](/wiki/MIT_Press "MIT Press") on behalf of the academy,[[5]](#cite_note-5) and has been open-access since January 2021.[[6]](#cite_note-6) The academy also conducts multidisciplinary public policy research.[[7]](#cite_note-7)

[Laurie L. Patton](/wiki/Laurie_L._Patton "Laurie L. Patton") has served as President of the Academy since January 2025.[[8]](#cite_note-8)

## History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=American_Academy_of_Arts_and_Sciences&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: History")]

The Academy was established by the [Massachusetts](/wiki/Massachusetts "Massachusetts") legislature on May 4, 1780, charted in order "to cultivate every art and science which may tend to advance the interest, honor, dignity, and happiness of a free, independent, and virtuous people."[[9]](#cite_note-9) The sixty-two incorporating fellows represented varying interests and high standing in the political, professional, and commercial sectors of the state. The first class of new members, chosen by the Academy in 1781, included [Benjamin Franklin](/wiki/Benjamin_Franklin "Benjamin Franklin") and [George Washington](/wiki/George_Washington "George Washington") as well as several international honorary members. The initial volume of Academy *Memoirs* appeared in 1785, and the *Proceedings* followed in 1846. In the 1950s, the Academy launched its journal *Daedalus*, reflecting its commitment to a broader intellectual and socially-oriented program.[[10]](#cite_note-10)

Since the second half of the twentieth century, independent research has become a central focus of the Academy. In the late 1950s, [arms control](/wiki/Arms_control "Arms control") emerged as one of its signature concerns. The Academy also served as the catalyst in establishing the [National Humanities Center](/wiki/National_Humanities_Center "National Humanities Center") in [North Carolina](/wiki/North_Carolina "North Carolina"). In the late 1990s, the Academy developed a new strategic plan, focusing on four major areas: science, technology, and global security; social policy and education; humanities and culture; and education. In 2002, the Academy established a visiting scholars program in association with [Harvard University](/wiki/Harvard_University "Harvard University"). More than 75 academic institutions from across the country have become Affiliates of the Academy to support this program and other Academy initiatives.[[11]](#cite_note-11)

The Academy has sponsored a number of awards and prizes,[[12]](#cite_note-12) throughout its history and has offered opportunities for fellowships and visiting scholars at the Academy.[[13]](#cite_note-13)

In July 2013, the [Boston Globe](/wiki/The_Boston_Globe "The Boston Globe") exposed then president [Leslie Berlowitz](/wiki/Leslie_Berlowitz "Leslie Berlowitz") for falsifying her credentials, faking a doctorate, and consistently mistreating her staff.[[14]](#cite_note-14) Berlowitz subsequently resigned.[[15]](#cite_note-15)[[16]](#cite_note-16)

## Projects

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=American_Academy_of_Arts_and_Sciences&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Projects")]

### The Humanities Indicators

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=American_Academy_of_Arts_and_Sciences&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: The Humanities Indicators")]

Main article: [Humanities Indicators](/wiki/Humanities_Indicators "Humanities Indicators")

A project of the Academy that equips researchers, policymakers, universities, foundations, museums, libraries, humanities councils, and other public institutions with [statistical](/wiki/Statistics "Statistics") tools for answering basic questions about primary and secondary humanities education, undergraduate and graduate education in the [humanities](/wiki/Humanities "Humanities"), the humanities workforce, levels and sources of program funding, public understanding and impact of the humanities, and other areas of concern in the humanities community.[[17]](#cite_note-17)[[18]](#cite_note-18)[[19]](#cite_note-19)[[20]](#cite_note-20) It is modeled on the Science and Engineering Indicators, published biennially by the [National Science Board](/wiki/National_Science_Board "National Science Board") as required by [Congress](/wiki/United_States_Congress "United States Congress").

### Classes and specialties

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=American_Academy_of_Arts_and_Sciences&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: Classes and specialties")]

As of 2023, membership is divided into five classes and thirty specialties.[[26]](#cite_note-26)

**Class I – Mathematical and physical sciences**

* Section 1. [Mathematics](/wiki/Mathematics "Mathematics"), [applied mathematics](/wiki/Applied_mathematics "Applied mathematics"), and [statistics](/wiki/Statistics "Statistics")
* Section 2. [Physics](/wiki/Physics "Physics")
* Section 3. [Chemistry](/wiki/Chemistry "Chemistry")
* Section 4. [Astronomy](/wiki/Astronomy "Astronomy"), [astrophysics](/wiki/Astrophysics "Astrophysics"), and [earth sciences](/wiki/Earth_science "Earth science")
* Section 5. Engineering and [technologies](/wiki/Technologies "Technologies")
* Section 6. [Computer sciences](/wiki/Computer_science "Computer science")

**Class II – Biological sciences**

* Section 1. [Biochemistry](/wiki/Biochemistry "Biochemistry"), [biophysics](/wiki/Biophysics "Biophysics"), and [molecular biology](/wiki/Molecular_biology "Molecular biology")
* Section 2. [Cellular](/wiki/Cell_biology "Cell biology") and [developmental biology](/wiki/Developmental_biology "Developmental biology")
* Section 3. [Neurosciences](/wiki/Neuroscience "Neuroscience")
* Section 4. [Evolution](/wiki/Evolutionary_biology "Evolutionary biology") and [ecology](/wiki/Ecology "Ecology")
* Section 5. [Medical sciences](/wiki/Medical_science "Medical science")

**Class III – Social and behavioral sciences**

* Section 1. [Psychological](/wiki/Psychological "Psychological") sciences
* Section 2. [Economics](/wiki/Economics "Economics")
* Section 3. [Political science](/wiki/Political_science "Political science")
* Section 4. [Law](/wiki/Law "Law")
* Section 5. [Archaeology](/wiki/Archaeology "Archaeology") and [anthropology](/wiki/Anthropology "Anthropology")
* Section 6. [Sociology](/wiki/Sociology "Sociology"), [demography](/wiki/Demography "Demography"), and [geography](/wiki/Geography "Geography")
* Section 7. [Education](/wiki/Education "Education")

**Class IV – Arts and humanities**

* Section 1. [Philosophy](/wiki/Philosophy "Philosophy")
* Section 2. [History](/wiki/History "History")
* Section 3. [Literature](/wiki/Literature "Literature") and [language studies](/wiki/Language_studies "Language studies")
* Section 4. Literature
* Section 5. [Visual arts](/wiki/Visual_arts "Visual arts")
* Section 6. [Performing arts](/wiki/Performing_arts "Performing arts")
* Section 7. [Religious studies](/wiki/Religious_studies "Religious studies")

**Class V – Public affairs, business, and administration**

* Section 1. [Journalism](/wiki/Journalism "Journalism"), [media](/wiki/Media_(communication) "Media (communication)"), and communications
* Section 2. Business, corporate, and [philanthropic](/wiki/Philanthropic "Philanthropic") leadership
* Section 3. Educational and academic leadership
* Section 4. Public affairs and [public policy](/wiki/Public_policy "Public policy")
* Section 5. Scientific, cultural, and nonprofit leadership
